```{r tbl-ipi-passthrough, eval=ipi_available}
  `vs Log-Linear` = c(fmt_pct(ipi_trend_core_ll),
                      fmt_pct(ipi_trend_dur_ll)),
  `2025 Change ` = c(fmt_pt(ipi_pt_core_simple),
                    fmt_pt(ipi_pt_dur_simple)),
  `vs LP Trend ` = c(fmt_pt(ipi_pt_core_lp),
                     fmt_pt(ipi_pt_dur_lp)),
  `vs Log-Linear ` = c(fmt_pt(ipi_pt_core_ll),
                       fmt_pt(ipi_pt_dur_ll))
)

render_table(
  ipi_passthrough,
  caption = paste0(fig_excel_title("T1b"), " (", data_month, "). ", fig_meta("T1b", "subtitle"), "."),
  align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
  header_above = c(" " = 1, "Inputs" = 3, "Price Changes" = 3, "Implied Passthrough" = 3)
)
```

```{r tbl-ipi-passthrough-na, eval=!ipi_available, results='asis'}
cat("*Imported PCE passthrough tables not available. Run import_price_index.R to generate.*\n")
```

```{r passthrough-notes, results='asis'}
cat("**Notes:**\n\n")
cat(paste0("- **LP (Local Projection)**: Price deviation from trend estimated using local projections ",
           "with recession/pandemic controls (1996--2024 estimation period). ",
           "See [", meth_text, "](", meth_link, ").\n"))
cat("- **Log-Linear**: Price deviation from simple log-linear trend fit to 2023--2024 data.\n")
cat(paste0("- **Simple**: Raw price change during 2025 through ", data_month_name,
           " with no trend adjustment.\n"))
if (!is.na(tau_core_recent)) {
  cat(sprintf(paste0("- Tariff rates are category-specific effective rates from USITC trade data: ",
                     "core goods %.1f%%, durables %.1f%% (vs %.1f%% baseline). ",
                     "Economy-wide effective rate as of %s: %.1f%%.\n"),
              tau_core_recent, tau_dur_recent, BASELINE_TARIFF_RATE * 100,
              data_month, latest_tariff))
}
```

---

### Import Prices

As noted above, there are other groups beyond US consumers that might bear the burden of tariffs. For some of these groups (e.g. firms and their shareholders) it is difficult to observe these effects directly. However, we can observe import prices: if foreign producers are willing to accept lower prices on their exports, the incidence of tariffs could fall, at least in part, on them. Below, we plot non-petroleum import prices, and find no evidence of a reduction in prices as a response to tariffs. This is a noisy series, so a response is possible: potentially import prices would have continued on their 2024 trajectory. But there is no clear evidence of a reduction in these prices. 

```{r fig-import-prices}
# Get base date dynamically
import_base_date <- get_base_date(import_prices, "date")
dec_base_import <- get_base_value(import_prices, "import_price_nonpetroleum", import_base_date)

# Index import prices to Dec 2024 = 100
import_indexed <- import_prices %>%
  filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
  mutate(import_idx = import_price_nonpetroleum / dec_base_import * 100)

# Calculate simple linear trend (2023-2024 estimation period)
import_trend <- import_indexed %>%
  calc_trend("import_idx", "2023-01-01", "2024-12-01")

import_trend %>%
  dplyr::select(date, Actual = import_idx, Trend = trend) %>%
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = value, linetype = series)) +
  geom_line(color = color_single, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Actual" = "solid", "Trend" = "dashed")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(96, 104)) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F9", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F9", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F9", "y_label"),
    linetype = NULL,
    caption = fig_caption("F9")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

*Note: October 2025 non-petroleum import price data were not published by BLS due to the [government shutdown](https://www.bls.gov/mxp/notices/2026/availability-of-oct-2025-mxpi-in-bls-database.htm). The October value is interpolated as the average of September and November 2025.*

---

## The Labor Market

```{r compute-emp-stats, include=FALSE}
# Pre-compute employment stats for inline text

# Unemployment rate change (nonfarm employment as proxy)
emp_latest <- employment %>% filter(!is.na(emp_nonfarm)) %>% filter(date == max(date))
emp_1yr_ago <- employment %>% filter(date == emp_latest$date %m-% months(12)) %>% slice(1)
unemp_latest_date <- format(emp_latest$date, "%B %Y")
nonfarm_yoy_change <- if (nrow(emp_1yr_ago) > 0) {
  (emp_latest$emp_nonfarm - emp_1yr_ago$emp_nonfarm)
} else { NA }
# Same YoY change one year earlier (for comparison)
emp_2yr_ago <- employment %>% filter(date == emp_latest$date %m-% months(24)) %>% slice(1)
nonfarm_yoy_change_prior <- if (nrow(emp_1yr_ago) > 0 && nrow(emp_2yr_ago) > 0) {
  (emp_1yr_ago$emp_nonfarm - emp_2yr_ago$emp_nonfarm)
} else { NA }

# Employment index stats (conditional on data availability)
if (tariff_emp_available) {
  # Index to Dec 2024 = 100
  emp_base <- get_base_value(tariff_emp, "I_t_recent", as.Date("2024-12-01"))
  emp_idx_latest <- tariff_emp %>% filter(date == max(date)) %>%
    mutate(idx = I_t_recent / emp_base * 100)
  emp_idx_dec <- 100  # by construction

  # YTD change
  emp_idx_ytd_pct <- emp_idx_latest$idx - 100

  # Compute trend for comparison
  emp_idx_series <- tariff_emp %>%
    filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
    mutate(idx = I_t_recent / emp_base * 100)
  emp_idx_with_trend <- calc_trend(emp_idx_series, "idx", "2023-01-01", "2024-12-01")
  emp_trend_latest <- emp_idx_with_trend %>% filter(date == max(date))
  emp_trend_predicted_change <- emp_trend_latest$trend - 100
  emp_shortfall_vs_trend <- emp_idx_ytd_pct - emp_trend_predicted_change

  # 2024 same-period comparison
  emp_latest_month <- month(max(tariff_emp$date))
  emp_dec_2023_base <- get_base_value(tariff_emp, "I_t_recent", as.Date("2023-12-01"))
  emp_same_month_2024 <- tariff_emp %>%
    filter(year(date) == 2024, month(date) == emp_latest_month) %>% slice(1)
  emp_2024_pct <- if (nrow(emp_same_month_2024) > 0 && !is.na(emp_dec_2023_base)) {
    (emp_same_month_2024$I_t_recent / emp_dec_2023_base - 1) * 100
  } else { NA }
  emp_shortfall_vs_2024 <- emp_idx_ytd_pct - emp_2024_pct
  emp_month_name <- format(max(tariff_emp$date), "%B")
} else {
  emp_idx_ytd_pct <- NA; emp_shortfall_vs_trend <- NA; emp_shortfall_vs_2024 <- NA
  emp_trend_predicted_change <- NA; emp_2024_pct <- NA
  emp_month_name <- "N/A"
}

# Manufacturing employment index stats
if (tariff_emp_mfg_available) {
  mfg_base <- get_base_value(tariff_emp_mfg, "I_t_recent", as.Date("2024-12-01"))
  mfg_idx_latest <- tariff_emp_mfg %>% filter(date == max(date)) %>%
    mutate(idx = I_t_recent / mfg_base * 100)
  mfg_idx_ytd_pct <- mfg_idx_latest$idx - 100

  mfg_idx_series <- tariff_emp_mfg %>%
    filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
    mutate(idx = I_t_recent / mfg_base * 100)
  mfg_idx_with_trend <- calc_trend(mfg_idx_series, "idx", "2023-01-01", "2024-12-01")
  mfg_trend_latest <- mfg_idx_with_trend %>% filter(date == max(date))
  mfg_trend_predicted_change <- mfg_trend_latest$trend - 100
} else {
  mfg_idx_ytd_pct <- NA; mfg_trend_predicted_change <- NA
}
```

Tariffs have the capacity to impact the labor market via different channels that operate over different time frames. Over the short run, prior to substantial effects on consumer or firm behavior, employment might not respond substantially to tariffs. However, over the medium-to-long term, the net effect of tariffs on employment depends on their overall effect on demand, the responses of the Federal Reserve to keep the economy at full-employment, and any positive (e.g. manufacturing) or negative (e.g. construction) sectoral effects that could emerge.

So far, there is no definitive indication of any effect of tariffs on the aggregate job market. Total non-farm employment as of `r unemp_latest_date` changed by `r safe_fmt(nonfarm_yoy_change / 1000, "%+.1f")`M jobs relative to the level of employment one year earlier. This is lower than that same statistic from one year prior (`r safe_fmt(nonfarm_yoy_change_prior / 1000, "%+.1f")`M).

However, the overall labor market might not be as affected as certain industries that are more exposed to tariffs. We therefore construct indices of tariff-sensitive employment, which measure employment weighted by industry-level tariff exposure.

```{r employment-equation, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
if (is_word) {
  cat("For industry `\\(j\\)`, commodity `\\(c\\)`, and month `\\(t\\)`:\n\n")
  cat("~~~\n")
  cat("\\begin{equation}\n")
  cat("  I_t = \\sum_j E_{j,t} \\times w_j, \\quad w_j = \\sum_c \\tau_c \\times R_{c,j} \\times s_c\n")
  cat("\\end{equation}\n")
  cat("~~~\n\n")
  cat("Where `\\(E_{j,t}\\)` is employment, `\\(\\tau_c\\)` is the tariff rate, `\\(R_{c,j}\\)` is the total requirement of commodity `\\(c\\)` per dollar of industry `\\(j\\)`'s output (from the BEA Leontief inverse), and `\\(s_c\\)` is the direct import share. This Leontief-adjusted weighting captures tariff cost pressure at every tier of the supply chain. The index is calculated using the most recent month's tariff rates. We calculate two versions of this index, one for all non-farm covered employment, and one for manufacturing.\n")
} else {
  cat("For industry $j$, commodity $c$, and month $t$:\n\n")
  cat("$$I_t = \\sum_j E_{j,t} \\times w_j, \\quad w_j = \\sum_c \\tau_c \\times R_{c,j} \\times s_c$$\n\n")
  cat("Where $E_{j,t}$ is employment, $\\tau_c$ is the tariff rate, $R_{c,j}$ is the total requirement of commodity $c$ per dollar of industry $j$'s output (from the BEA Leontief inverse), and $s_c$ is the direct import share. This Leontief-adjusted weighting captures tariff cost pressure at every tier of the supply chain. The index is calculated using the most recent month's tariff rates. We calculate two versions of this index, one for all non-farm covered employment, and one for manufacturing.\n")
}
```

Looking at the overall index, there is some evidence of weakening employment during 2025: the index changed by `r safe_fmt(emp_idx_ytd_pct)`% during 2025 through `r emp_month_name`, which is `r safe_fmt(abs(emp_shortfall_vs_trend))`% less than the pre-2025 trend would have predicted, and `r safe_fmt(abs(emp_shortfall_vs_2024))`% less than the growth rate in 2024 over the same period. When we just look at tariff-sensitive manufacturing employment, the manufacturing index changed by `r safe_fmt(mfg_idx_ytd_pct)`%, versus the trend's `r safe_fmt(mfg_trend_predicted_change)`% predicted change. In other words, there are some indications of weakness in tariff-exposed employment overall, but not in the manufacturing sector. It is worth noting, however, that both of these comparisons are illustrative only, and more rigorous work is necessary to determine the short-term effects of tariffs on employment.

```{r fig-tariff-emp-combined, eval=tariff_emp_available, fig.height=7}
# Fixed base: December 2024 = 100
emp_idx_base_date <- as.Date("2024-12-01")

# Get base values for indexing
dec_base_recent <- get_base_value(tariff_emp, "I_t_recent", emp_idx_base_date)

# Index employment indices to Dec 2024 = 100
tariff_emp_indexed <- tariff_emp %>%
  filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
  mutate(
    idx_recent = I_t_recent / dec_base_recent * 100
  )

trend_recent <- tariff_emp_indexed %>%
  calc_trend("idx_recent", "2023-01-01", "2024-12-01") %>%
  dplyr::select(date, actual = idx_recent, trend, vs_trend)


# Plot single series
ggplot(trend_recent, aes(x = date)) +
  # Trend line
  geom_line(aes(y = trend), color = color_single, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  # Actual values
  geom_line(aes(y = actual), color = color_single, linewidth = 1) +
  # Reference lines
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(96, 104)) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F10", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F10", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F10", "y_label"),
    caption = fig_caption("F10")
  )
```

```{r fig-tariff-emp-na, eval=!tariff_emp_available, results='asis'}
cat("*Tariff-exposed employment index not available. Run employment_index.R with input files to generate.*\n")
```

```{r fig-tariff-emp-mfg-combined, eval=tariff_emp_mfg_available, fig.height=7}
# Fixed base: December 2024 = 100
mfg_emp_idx_base_date <- as.Date("2024-12-01")

# Get base values for indexing
dec_base_mfg_recent <- get_base_value(tariff_emp_mfg, "I_t_recent", mfg_emp_idx_base_date)

# Index employment indices to Dec 2024 = 100
tariff_emp_mfg_indexed <- tariff_emp_mfg %>%
  filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
  mutate(
    idx_recent = I_t_recent / dec_base_mfg_recent * 100
  )

trend_mfg_recent <- tariff_emp_mfg_indexed %>%
  calc_trend("idx_recent", "2023-01-01", "2024-12-01") %>%
  dplyr::select(date, actual = idx_recent, trend, vs_trend)

# Plot single series
ggplot(trend_mfg_recent, aes(x = date)) +
  # Trend line
  geom_line(aes(y = trend), color = color_single, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  # Actual values
  geom_line(aes(y = actual), color = color_single, linewidth = 1) +
  # Reference lines
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(96, 104)) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F11", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F11", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F11", "y_label"),
    caption = fig_caption("F11")
  )
```

```{r tbl-emp-index-summary, eval=FALSE}
# NOTE: This table is disabled pending finalization of the employment index display format.
# Summary table with 2 columns (all industries and manufacturing)
latest_all <- tariff_emp %>% filter(date == max(date))
latest_mfg <- tariff_emp_mfg %>% filter(date == max(date))

emp_index_summary <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Latest Date",
    "Employment Covered",
    "Industries in Index",
    "I_t (tariff-weighted employment)",
    "Deviation from Trend"
  ),
  `All Industries` = c(
    format(latest_all$date, "%B %Y"),
    format(round(latest_all$total_employment, 0), big.mark = ","),
    as.character(latest_all$n_industries),
    format(round(latest_all$I_t_recent, 0), big.mark = ","),
    sprintf("%+.2f%%", trend_recent %>% filter(date == max(date)) %>% pull(vs_trend))
  ),
  `Manufacturing` = c(
    format(latest_mfg$date, "%B %Y"),
    format(round(latest_mfg$total_employment, 0), big.mark = ","),
    as.character(latest_mfg$n_industries),
    format(round(latest_mfg$I_t_recent, 0), big.mark = ","),
    sprintf("%+.2f%%", trend_mfg_recent %>% filter(date == max(date)) %>% pull(vs_trend))
  )
)

render_table(
  emp_index_summary,
  caption = paste0(fig_excel_title("T_Emp"), ". ", fig_meta("T_Emp", "subtitle"), "."),
  align = c("l", "r", "r")
)
```

```{r fig-tariff-emp-mfg-na, eval=!tariff_emp_mfg_available, results='asis'}
cat("*Manufacturing employment index not available. Run employment_index.R with input files to generate.*\n")
```

---

## Industrial Output

```{r compute-ip-stats, include=FALSE}
# Pre-compute industrial production stats for inline text
ip_base_val <- get_base_value(industrial_production, "ip_manufacturing", as.Date("2024-12-01"))
ip_latest <- industrial_production %>% filter(!is.na(ip_manufacturing)) %>% filter(date == max(date))
ip_ytd_pct <- (ip_latest$ip_manufacturing / ip_base_val - 1) * 100
ip_month_name <- format(ip_latest$date, "%B")
```

Building on the analysis of tariff-sensitive manufacturing employment above, we also consider the evolution of industrial production in this same sector. The manufacturing industrial production index is constructed by the Federal Reserve Board and measures real output of manufacturing industries. We plot the index from 2021 onward, and find that during 2025 through `r ip_month_name`, the index has changed by `r safe_fmt(ip_ytd_pct, "%+.1f")`%, though it is worth noting it is possible this is due to mean-reversion rather than to any tariff-related effect. In this case, the 2-year pre-trend plotted below may not be the appropriate counterfactual. 

```{r fig-industrial-prod}
# Base date: December 2024 = 100
dec_base_ip <- get_base_value(industrial_production, "ip_manufacturing", as.Date("2024-12-01"))

ip_indexed <- industrial_production %>%
  filter(date >= "2021-01-01") %>%
  mutate(ip_idx = ip_manufacturing / dec_base_ip * 100)

ip_trend <- ip_indexed %>%
  calc_trend("ip_idx", "2023-01-01", "2024-12-01")

ip_trend %>%
  dplyr::select(date, Actual = ip_idx, Trend = trend) %>%
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = value, linetype = series)) +
  geom_line(color = color_single, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Actual" = "solid", "Trend" = "dashed")) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F12", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F12", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F12", "y_label"),
    linetype = NULL,
    caption = fig_caption("F12")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

---

## The Strength of the US Dollar

```{r compute-fx-stats, include=FALSE}
# Pre-compute exchange rate stats for inline text
# Using daily BIS NEER indices (higher = currency appreciation)

# December 2024 daily averages (base period)
fx_dec_avg <- fx_daily %>%
  filter(year(date) == 2024, month(date) == 12) %>%
  summarize(
    usd = mean(usd_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    cny = mean(cny_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    mxn = mean(mxn_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    cad = mean(cad_neer, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

# Last full month daily averages
last_full_month <- fx_daily %>%
  mutate(ym = floor_date(date, "month")) %>%
  filter(ym < floor_date(max(date), "month")) %>%
  filter(ym == max(ym))

fx_latest_avg <- last_full_month %>%
  summarize(
    usd = mean(usd_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    cny = mean(cny_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    mxn = mean(mxn_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    cad = mean(cad_neer, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

fx_latest_date <- format(max(last_full_month$date), "%B %Y")

# Percent changes in NEER (higher = appreciation)
twi_vs_dec <- (fx_latest_avg$usd / fx_dec_avg$usd - 1) * 100
cny_vs_dec <- (fx_latest_avg$cny / fx_dec_avg$cny - 1) * 100
mxn_vs_dec <- (fx_latest_avg$mxn / fx_dec_avg$mxn - 1) * 100
cad_vs_dec <- (fx_latest_avg$cad / fx_dec_avg$cad - 1) * 100

# Long-run average TWI (2000-2024, from long-history series)
twi_longrun_avg <- twi_long %>%
  filter(date >= "2000-01-01", date <= "2024-12-01") %>%
  summarize(avg = mean(twdol, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% pull(avg)
twi_dec_2024 <- twi_long %>%
  filter(year(date) == 2024, month(date) == 12) %>% slice(1)
twi_dec_vs_longrun <- (twi_dec_2024$twdol / twi_longrun_avg - 1) * 100
```

A conventional assumption in economics is that tariffs, when faced with incomplete retaliation from other countries, will cause either an appreciation in the levying country's currency and/or a depreciation in the currencies of the other countries. This is because the relative demand for the levying country's currency rises as their consumers and businesses buy fewer imports. The [Budget Lab's tariff modeling](https://budgetlab.yale.edu/research/short-run-effects-2025-tariffs-so-far) has assumed that currency adjustments offset roughly one-third of the cost of US tariffs to US consumers & businesses without foreign retaliation.

The reality, however, has been different so far. As of `r fx_latest_date`, the US dollar is `r safe_fmt(abs(twi_vs_dec))`% `r if (!is.na(twi_vs_dec) && twi_vs_dec < 0) "weaker" else "stronger"` than its December 2024 average. There's also been inconsistent depreciation among the targets of US tariffs. China's yuan is `r safe_fmt(abs(cny_vs_dec))`% `r if (!is.na(cny_vs_dec) && cny_vs_dec >= 0) "stronger" else "weaker"` than its December 2024 average, while Canada's dollar is `r safe_fmt(abs(cad_vs_dec))`% `r if (!is.na(cad_vs_dec) && cad_vs_dec >= 0) "stronger" else "weaker"` over the same period. Mexico's peso is `r safe_fmt(abs(mxn_vs_dec))`% `r if (!is.na(mxn_vs_dec) && mxn_vs_dec >= 0) "stronger" else "weaker"` than December 2024, though this is not out of the range of pre-2025 variation, making it difficult to establish any causal claim.

These currency movements will affect the price of imports for consumers. While stronger currencies make imported goods cheaper for residents, weaker currencies have the opposite effect, making imports more expensive. A `r if (!is.na(twi_vs_dec) && twi_vs_dec < 0) "weakening" else "strengthening"` US dollar `r if (!is.na(twi_vs_dec) && twi_vs_dec < 0) "exacerbates the price impact of tariffs by making all imports more expensive in dollar terms" else "partially offsets the price impact of tariffs by making imports cheaper in dollar terms"`.

It is unclear what explains these currency movements. As a first order effect, US tariffs may indeed be putting upward pressure on the US dollar but other factors, like a deteriorating economic outlook, shifting views on the credibility of US institutions, the role of the US dollar in the global economy, and expectations of lower interest rates, are likely exerting downward pressure. The real trade-weighted dollar also entered 2025 roughly `r safe_fmt(twi_dec_vs_longrun)`% stronger than its long-run average level (2000--2024), so mean reversion may also be a factor in dollar movement.


```{r fig-currencies, eval=fx_daily_available}
# Compute December 2024 daily averages as base (= 100)
dec_2024_avg <- fx_daily %>%
  filter(year(date) == 2024, month(date) == 12) %>%
  summarize(
    usd_base = mean(usd_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    mxn_base = mean(mxn_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    cad_base = mean(cad_neer, na.rm = TRUE),
    cny_base = mean(cny_neer, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

# Index each series to December 2024 average = 100
# BIS nominal effective exchange rate (NEER) indices: higher = currency appreciation
# No inversion needed -- series already in "higher = stronger" convention
fx_daily_indexed <- fx_daily %>%
  mutate(
    `US Dollar` = 100 * (usd_neer / dec_2024_avg$usd_base),
    `Mexico (MXN)` = 100 * (mxn_neer / dec_2024_avg$mxn_base),
    `Canada (CAD)` = 100 * (cad_neer / dec_2024_avg$cad_base),
    `China (CNY)` = 100 * (cny_neer / dec_2024_avg$cny_base)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(date, `US Dollar`, `Mexico (MXN)`, `Canada (CAD)`, `China (CNY)`) %>%
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "currency", values_to = "index")

fx_daily_indexed %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = index, color = currency)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("US Dollar" = tbl_colors[4],
                                 "Mexico (MXN)" = tbl_colors[1],
                                 "Canada (CAD)" = tbl_colors[2],
                                 "China (CNY)" = tbl_colors[3])) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F13", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F13", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F13", "y_label"),
    color = NULL,
    caption = fig_caption("F13")
  )
```

---

## Imports & Exports

```{r compute-trade-stats, include=FALSE}
# Pre-compute trade stats for inline text

# Compute implicit price deflator at Dec 2024 to convert from chained 2017$ to ~2025$
trade_dec_2024 <- trade_flows %>%
  filter(year(date) == 2024, month(date) == 12) %>% slice(1)
imports_deflator <- trade_dec_2024$imports_nominal / trade_dec_2024$imports_real
exports_deflator <- trade_dec_2024$exports_nominal / trade_dec_2024$exports_real

# Fit trends first (needed before prose)
trade_for_pre <- trade_flows %>% filter(date >= "2023-01-01")
imports_pre <- trade_for_pre %>% filter(date < "2025-01-01")
exports_pre <- trade_for_pre %>% filter(date < "2025-01-01")
imports_pre$time_idx <- as.numeric(imports_pre$date - min(imports_pre$date))
exports_pre$time_idx <- as.numeric(exports_pre$date - min(exports_pre$date))
imports_lm_pre <- lm(imports_real ~ time_idx, data = imports_pre)
exports_lm_pre <- lm(exports_real ~ time_idx, data = exports_pre)

trade_pre_trend <- trade_for_pre %>%
  mutate(
    time_idx = as.numeric(date - min(date)),
    imports_trend = predict(imports_lm_pre, newdata = data.frame(time_idx = time_idx)),
    exports_trend = predict(exports_lm_pre, newdata = data.frame(time_idx = time_idx)),
    imports_vs_trend = (imports_real / imports_trend - 1) * 100,
    exports_vs_trend = (exports_real / exports_trend - 1) * 100
  )

# March 2025 import surge (change in deviation from Dec 2024 to Mar 2025)
mar_2025 <- trade_pre_trend %>% filter(date == as.Date("2025-03-01"))
dec_2024_trade <- trade_pre_trend %>% filter(date == as.Date("2024-12-01"))
mar_imports_vs_trend <- if (nrow(mar_2025) > 0 && nrow(dec_2024_trade) > 0) {
  mar_2025$imports_vs_trend - dec_2024_trade$imports_vs_trend
} else NA
mar_imports_excess <- if (nrow(mar_2025) > 0 && nrow(dec_2024_trade) > 0) {
  ((mar_2025$imports_real - mar_2025$imports_trend) -
   (dec_2024_trade$imports_real - dec_2024_trade$imports_trend)) * imports_deflator / 1000
} else NA

# Post-April average
post_apr <- trade_pre_trend %>% filter(date >= "2025-04-01")
post_apr_avg_imports_vs_trend <- if (nrow(post_apr) > 0) mean(post_apr$imports_vs_trend, na.rm = TRUE) else NA

# Latest values
trade_latest <- trade_pre_trend %>% filter(date == max(date))
trade_latest_date <- format(trade_latest$date, "%B %Y")
exports_latest_vs_trend <- trade_latest$exports_vs_trend
exports_dir <- if (!is.na(exports_latest_vs_trend) && exports_latest_vs_trend > 0) "above" else "below"

# Cumulative import gap since Dec 2024
cum_gap <- trade_pre_trend %>%
  filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
  mutate(
    monthly_gap = (imports_real - imports_trend) * imports_deflator / 1000,
    cumulative_gap = cumsum(monthly_gap)
  )
cum_imports_total <- cum_gap %>% filter(date == max(date)) %>% pull(cumulative_gap)

```

A country's trade deficit, or the amount by which their imports from other countries exceed their exports to those countries, can be impacted by tariffs if the higher prices of non-domestic goods induces a shift in purchasing by consumers and businesses. However, if the currency of the levying country appreciates as a result of tariffs, this will drive down the relative price of imports, meaning that any sort of persistent reduction in the trade deficit due to tariffs is unlikely in the long run. We are still in the early days of any such transition, and, as noted above, the changes in the value of the dollar might be driven by other factors, meaning that the expected offsetting effects of a (relatively) stronger dollar might not materialize. It is therefore difficult to evaluate the medium-to-long-term effects of tariffs on the trade deficit.

There are, however, some notable dynamics in the short-term reaction of imports over the last year. First, real imports grew `r safe_fmt(mar_imports_vs_trend)`% over the pre-2025 trend between December 2024 and March 2025, equal to $`r safe_fmt(mar_imports_excess)` billion in real 2025 dollars, as consumers and businesses sought to make purchases ahead of tariffs taking effect. Starting in April, imports declined, and as of `r trade_latest_date` imports have been on average `r safe_fmt(post_apr_avg_imports_vs_trend)`% `r if (!is.na(post_apr_avg_imports_vs_trend) && post_apr_avg_imports_vs_trend < 0) "below" else "above"` the pre-2025 trend. Real exports `r if (!is.na(exports_latest_vs_trend) && exports_latest_vs_trend < 0) "fell" else "rose"` more modestly (in absolute terms) than imports, and are `r safe_fmt(abs(exports_latest_vs_trend))`% `r exports_dir` trend as of `r trade_latest_date`.

Cumulatively, as of `r trade_latest_date`, cumulative imports are still `r if (!is.na(cum_imports_total) && cum_imports_total > 0) "up" else "down"` by $`r safe_fmt(abs(cum_imports_total))` billion (real 2025 USD) from December 2024 relative to trend. This has implications for the discussion of stockpiling: this very rough measure of imports implies that the ability of firms to rely on pre-tariff stocks of goods will degrade shortly. 

```{r fig-imports-exports}
# Reuse trade trends computed in compute-trade-stats chunk above
trade_with_trend <- trade_pre_trend

# Convert from chained 2017$ to ~2025$ using implicit price deflators
trade_with_trend <- trade_with_trend %>%
  mutate(
    imports_real_2025 = imports_real * imports_deflator,
    imports_trend_2025 = imports_trend * imports_deflator,
    exports_real_2025 = exports_real * exports_deflator,
    exports_trend_2025 = exports_trend * exports_deflator
  )

# Prepare data for plotting - show levels in billions of real 2025 USD
trade_plot <- trade_with_trend %>%
  dplyr::select(date,
         Imports = imports_real_2025,
         `Imports (Trend)` = imports_trend_2025,
         Exports = exports_real_2025,
         `Exports (Trend)` = exports_trend_2025) %>%
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(
    type = ifelse(grepl("Imports", series), "Imports", "Exports"),
    line_type = ifelse(grepl("Trend", series), "Trend", "Actual")
  )

ggplot(trade_plot, aes(x = date, y = value / 1000, color = type, linetype = line_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Imports" = color_series1, "Exports" = color_series2)) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Actual" = "solid", "Trend" = "dashed"),
                        guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F14", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F14", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F14", "y_label"),
    color = NULL,
    caption = fig_caption("F14")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), labels = dollar_format(prefix = "$", suffix = "B"))

# Store for later use
imports_trend <- trade_with_trend %>%
  mutate(imports_idx = imports_real, trend = imports_trend,
         vs_trend = (imports_real / imports_trend - 1) * 100)
exports_trend <- trade_with_trend %>%
  mutate(exports_idx = exports_real, trend = exports_trend,
         vs_trend = (exports_real / exports_trend - 1) * 100)
```

```{r fig-trade-deviation, fig.height=10}
# Calculate percent deviation from trend
trade_deviation <- trade_with_trend %>%
  filter(date >= "2024-01-01") %>%
  mutate(
    imports_vs_trend = (imports_real / imports_trend - 1) * 100,
    exports_vs_trend = (exports_real / exports_trend - 1) * 100
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(date, `Imports` = imports_vs_trend, `Exports` = exports_vs_trend) %>%
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "pct_deviation")

p1 <- ggplot(trade_deviation, aes(x = date, y = pct_deviation, fill = series)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Imports" = color_series1, "Exports" = color_series2)) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F15a", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F15a", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F15a", "y_label"),
    fill = NULL
  )

# Panel B: Cumulative imports gap above/below trend (in billions, 2025 USD)
cumulative_trade <- trade_with_trend %>%
  filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
  mutate(
    imports_gap = (imports_real_2025 - imports_trend_2025) / 1000,
    cumul_imports = cumsum(imports_gap)
  )

p2 <- ggplot(cumulative_trade, aes(x = date, y = cumul_imports)) +
  geom_line(color = color_series1, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("F15b", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F15b", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("F15b", "y_label"),
    caption = fig_caption("F15b")
  )

# Keep cumulative_imports for excel export compatibility
cumulative_imports <- cumulative_trade %>%
  dplyr::select(date, imports_real, imports_trend,
         monthly_gap = imports_gap, cumulative_gap = cumul_imports)

# Combine panels
p1 / p2
```

---

## Data Sources

All data in this report is pulled directly from Haver Analytics databases:

- **USECON**: U.S. Economic data
- **USNA**: National Accounts (PCE price indices)
- **USTRADE**: Trade data by country
- **USINT**: International trade
- **LABOR**: Employment by industry

---

## Appendix {#appendix}

```{r tbl-passthrough-jun}
passthrough_data_jun <- tibble(
  Category = c("Core Goods", "Durables"),
  `Import Share` = c(sprintf("%.1f%%", IMPORT_SHARE_CORE_GOODS * 100),
                     sprintf("%.1f%%", IMPORT_SHARE_DURABLES * 100)),
  `Tariff Increase` = c(sprintf("%.1f pp", jun_tariff_increase * 100),
                        sprintf("%.1f pp", jun_tariff_increase * 100)),
  `Expected Effect` = c(sprintf("%.1f%%", jun_expected_core),
                        sprintf("%.1f%%", jun_expected_dur)),
  `2025 Change` = c(fmt_pct(ytd_core_jun),
                   fmt_pct(ytd_dur_jun)),
  `vs LP Trend` = c(fmt_pct(trend_core_lp_jun),
                    fmt_pct(trend_dur_lp_jun)),
  `vs Log-Linear` = c(fmt_pct(trend_core_ll_jun),
                      fmt_pct(trend_dur_ll_jun)),
  `2025 Change ` = c(fmt_pt(pt_core_simple_jun),
                    fmt_pt(pt_dur_simple_jun)),
  `vs LP Trend ` = c(fmt_pt(pt_core_lp_jun),
                     fmt_pt(pt_dur_lp_jun)),
  `vs Log-Linear ` = c(fmt_pt(pt_core_ll_jun),
                       fmt_pt(pt_dur_ll_jun))
)

render_table(
  passthrough_data_jun,
  caption = paste0(fig_excel_title("TA1"), ". ", fig_meta("TA1", "subtitle"),
                   ": ", fmt_pct(jun_tariff), "."),
  align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
  header_above = c(" " = 1, "Inputs" = 3, "Price Changes" = 3, "Implied Passthrough" = 3)
)
```

```{r tbl-passthrough}
passthrough_data <- tibble(
  Category = c("Core Goods", "Durables"),
  `Import Share` = c(sprintf("%.1f%%", IMPORT_SHARE_CORE_GOODS * 100),
                     sprintf("%.1f%%", IMPORT_SHARE_DURABLES * 100)),
  `Tariff Increase` = c(sprintf("%.1f pp", tariff_increase * 100),
                        sprintf("%.1f pp", tariff_increase * 100)),
  `Expected Effect` = c(sprintf("%.1f%%", expected_core),
                        sprintf("%.1f%%", expected_dur)),
  `2025 Change` = c(sprintf("%.1f%%", ytd_core),
                   sprintf("%.1f%%", ytd_dur)),
  `vs LP Trend` = c(fmt_pct(trend_core_lp),
                    fmt_pct(trend_dur_lp)),
  `vs Log-Linear` = c(fmt_pct(trend_core_ll),
                      fmt_pct(trend_dur_ll)),
  `2025 Change ` = c(fmt_pt(pt_core_simple),
                    fmt_pt(pt_dur_simple)),
  `vs LP Trend ` = c(fmt_pt(pt_core_lp),
                     fmt_pt(pt_dur_lp)),
  `vs Log-Linear ` = c(fmt_pt(pt_core_ll),
                       fmt_pt(pt_dur_ll))
)

render_table(
  passthrough_data,
  caption = paste0(fig_excel_title("TA2"), " (", data_month, "). ", fig_meta("TA2", "subtitle"), "."),
  align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
  header_above = c(" " = 1, "Inputs" = 3, "Price Changes" = 3, "Implied Passthrough" = 3)
)
```

**Note:** Tables A1--A2 use overall PCE price indices (domestic + imported) rather than the import-weighted index used in the main text Tables 1a--1b.

```{r fig-pce-goods-ll}
# Index log-linear results to Dec 2024 = 100 for plotting
core_ll_base <- core_loglinear_pt %>%
  filter(date == as.Date("2024-12-01")) %>%
  pull(pce_core_goods)
dur_ll_base <- dur_loglinear_pt %>%
  filter(date == as.Date("2024-12-01")) %>%
  pull(pce_durables)

ll_combined <- core_loglinear_pt %>%
  filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
  mutate(core_goods_idx = pce_core_goods / core_ll_base * 100) %>%
  dplyr::select(date, core_goods_idx, core_trend = trend, core_lower = trend_lower,
         core_upper = trend_upper, is_forecast) %>%
  left_join(
    dur_loglinear_pt %>%
      filter(date >= "2023-01-01") %>%
      mutate(durables_idx = pce_durables / dur_ll_base * 100) %>%
      dplyr::select(date, durables_idx, dur_trend = trend, dur_lower = trend_lower,
             dur_upper = trend_upper),
    by = "date"
  )

ggplot(ll_combined, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_ribbon(data = filter(ll_combined, is_forecast),
              aes(ymin = core_lower, ymax = core_upper),
              fill = color_series1, alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_ribbon(data = filter(ll_combined, is_forecast),
              aes(ymin = dur_lower, ymax = dur_upper),
              fill = color_series2, alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(data = filter(ll_combined, date >= "2024-12-01"),
            aes(y = core_trend, color = "Core Goods", linetype = "Trend"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(data = filter(ll_combined, date >= "2024-12-01"),
            aes(y = dur_trend, color = "Durables", linetype = "Trend"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = core_goods_idx, color = "Core Goods", linetype = "Actual"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = durables_idx, color = "Durables", linetype = "Actual"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2025-01-01"), linetype = "dashed", color = tbl_gray) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Core Goods" = color_series1, "Durables" = color_series2)) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Actual" = "solid", "Trend" = "dashed"),
                        guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(96, 104)) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("FA1", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("FA1", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("FA1", "y_label"),
    color = NULL,
    caption = fig_caption("FA1")
  )
```

```{r fig-deviation-ll}
ll_deviations <- ll_combined %>%
  filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
  mutate(
    core_goods_vs_trend = (core_goods_idx / core_trend - 1) * 100,
    durables_vs_trend = (durables_idx / dur_trend - 1) * 100
  )

ll_deviations %>%
  dplyr::select(date, `Core Goods` = core_goods_vs_trend, `Durables` = durables_vs_trend) %>%
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = value, fill = series)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Core Goods" = color_series1, "Durables" = color_series2)) +
  labs(
    title = fig_meta("FA2", "title"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("FA2", "subtitle"),
    x = NULL,
    y = fig_meta("FA2", "y_label"),
    fill = NULL,
    caption = fig_caption("FA2")
  )
```

---

## Export Data to Excel

```{r export-excel, include=FALSE}
# openxlsx is listed in REQUIRED_PACKAGES in run_all.R
library(openxlsx)

# Create workbook
wb <- createWorkbook()

# --- TBL House Styles (Aptos Narrow 11pt throughout) ---
titleStyle    <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11,
                             textDecoration = "bold")
metaStyle     <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11)
headerStyle   <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11,
                             textDecoration = "bold", halign = "center",
                             border = "bottom", borderStyle = "thin")
dataStyleNum  <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11,
                             halign = "center", numFmt = "0.00")
dataStyleDate <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11,
                             halign = "center", numFmt = "mmm-yy")
dataStyleChar <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11,
                             halign = "center")
tocLinkStyle  <- createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11)

# Track sheet info for Data TOC
sheet_info <- tibble::tibble(sheet_name = character(), title = character())

# Helper: add a data sheet in TBL house style (7-row header structure)
add_tbl_sheet <- function(wb, sheet_name, data, title, subtitle, source,
                          notes = "", date_format = NULL) {
  tryCatch({
    addWorksheet(wb, sheet_name, gridLines = FALSE)

    # Row 1: Title (bold)
    writeData(wb, sheet_name, title, startRow = 1, startCol = 1)
    addStyle(wb, sheet_name, titleStyle, rows = 1, cols = 1)

    # Row 2: Subtitle
    writeData(wb, sheet_name, paste0("Subtitle: ", subtitle),
              startRow = 2, startCol = 1)
    addStyle(wb, sheet_name, metaStyle, rows = 2, cols = 1)

    # Track current row for conditional notes
    current_row <- 3

    # Notes (only if provided)
    if (nzchar(notes)) {
      writeData(wb, sheet_name, paste0("Notes: ", notes),
                startRow = current_row, startCol = 1)
      addStyle(wb, sheet_name, metaStyle, rows = current_row, cols = 1)
      current_row <- current_row + 1
    }

    # Source
    writeData(wb, sheet_name, paste0("Source: ", source),
              startRow = current_row, startCol = 1)
    addStyle(wb, sheet_name, metaStyle, rows = current_row, cols = 1)

    # Data starts 2 rows after source (blank separator + header row)
    data_start <- current_row + 2
    writeData(wb, sheet_name, data, startRow = data_start, startCol = 1)

    ncols <- ncol(data)
    nrows <- nrow(data)

    # Style column headers
    addStyle(wb, sheet_name, headerStyle, rows = data_start, cols = 1:ncols,
             gridExpand = TRUE)

    # Style data rows by column type
    if (nrows > 0) {
      for (j in seq_len(ncols)) {
        col_data <- data[[j]]
        if (inherits(col_data, "Date") || inherits(col_data, "POSIXct")) {
          ds <- if (!is.null(date_format)) {
            createStyle(fontName = "Aptos Narrow", fontSize = 11,
                        halign = "center", numFmt = date_format)
          } else dataStyleDate
          addStyle(wb, sheet_name, ds,
                   rows = (data_start + 1):(data_start + nrows), cols = j, gridExpand = TRUE)
        } else if (is.numeric(col_data)) {
          addStyle(wb, sheet_name, dataStyleNum,
                   rows = (data_start + 1):(data_start + nrows), cols = j, gridExpand = TRUE)
        } else {
          addStyle(wb, sheet_name, dataStyleChar,
                   rows = (data_start + 1):(data_start + nrows), cols = j, gridExpand = TRUE)
        }
      }
    }

    # Black bottom border after last row of data
    if (nrows > 0) {
      bottomBorder <- createStyle(border = "bottom", borderStyle = "thin", borderColour = "#000000")
      addStyle(wb, sheet_name, bottomBorder,
               rows = data_start + nrows, cols = 1:ncols,
               gridExpand = TRUE, stack = TRUE)
    }

    # Auto column widths
    setColWidths(wb, sheet_name, cols = 1:ncols, widths = "auto")

    # Track for TOC
    sheet_info <<- bind_rows(sheet_info, tibble::tibble(
      sheet_name = sheet_name,
      title = title
    ))

    TRUE
  }, error = function(e) {
    message(paste("Could not add sheet", sheet_name, ":", e$message))
    FALSE
  })
}

# Figure 1: Customs Duty Revenue
fig1_data <- revenue_real %>%
  dplyr::select("Date"= date, 
                "Customs duties (nominal USD)" = customs_duties, 
                "Customs duties (2025 USD)" = customs_duties_real) %>%
  mutate("2022-2024 average customs duties (2025 USD)" = pre_2025_avg)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F1", fig1_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F1"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F1", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F1", "source"),
  notes    = fig_meta("F1", "notes"))

# Figure 2: Effective Tariff Rate
fig2_data <- tariff_revenue %>%
  filter(date >= "2020-01-01") %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date, 
                "Customs duties (nominal USD)" = customs_duties, 
                "Import values (nominal USD)" = imports_value, 
                "Effective tariff rate (%)" = effective_rate)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F2", fig2_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F2"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F2", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F2", "source"))

# Figure 3: Daily Effective Tariff Rate
if (daily_etr_available) {
  fig3_data <- daily_etr %>%
    filter(date <= Sys.Date()) %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Revision" = revision,
                  "Weighted ETR" = weighted_etr,
                  "Weighted ETR (additional)" = weighted_etr_additional,
                  "Matched imports ($B)" = matched_imports_b,
                  "Total imports ($B)" = total_imports_b)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F3", fig3_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F3"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F3", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F3", "source"),
    notes    = fig_meta("F3", "notes"),
    date_format = "yyyy-mm-dd")
}

# Figure 4: Daily ETR by Authority
if (daily_etr_available) {
  fig4_data <- daily_auth %>%
    filter(date <= Sys.Date()) %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Revision" = revision,
                  "Section 232 ETR" = etr_232,
                  "Section 301 ETR" = etr_301,
                  "IEEPA ETR" = etr_ieepa,
                  "Fentanyl ETR" = etr_fentanyl,
                  "Section 122 ETR" = etr_s122)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F4", fig4_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F4"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F4", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F4", "source"),
    notes    = fig_meta("F4", "notes"),
    date_format = "yyyy-mm-dd")
}

# Figure 5: PCE Prices with Trend
fig3_data <- pce_combined %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
         "Core goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = core_goods_idx,
         "Core goods trend (pre-2025)" = core_trend,
         "Core goods trend, lower bound" = core_lower,
         "Core goods trend, upper bound" = core_upper,
         "Durable goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = durables_idx,
         "Durable goods trend (pre-2025)" = dur_trend,
         "Durable goods trend, lower bound" = dur_lower,
         "Durable goods trend, upper bound" = dur_upper,
         "Forecast indicator" = is_forecast)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F5", fig3_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F5"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F5", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F5", "source"))

# Figure 6: Deviation from Trend
fig4_data <- pce_for_fig4 %>%
  filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date, 
                "Core goods deviation from trend (%)" = core_goods_vs_trend,
                "Durable goods deviation from trend (%)" = dur_vs_trend)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F6", fig4_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F6"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F6", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F6", "source"))

# Table 1a: IPI Passthrough (June 2025) - main text
if (ipi_available && exists("ipi_passthrough_jun")) {
  
  tab1a_data <- ipi_passthrough_jun %>%
    dplyr::select(Category, 
                  "Import share" = "Import Share", 
                  "Tariff increase (pp)" = "Tariff Increase", 
                  "Expected price change under 100% passthrough" = "Expected Effect", 
                  "Price change, 2025 change" = "2025 Change", 
                  "Price change, vs LP trend" = "vs LP Trend", 
                  "Price change, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear",   
                  "Passthrough, via 2025 change" = "2025 Change ", 
                  "Passthrough, vs LP trend" =  "vs LP Trend ",
                  "vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear "
                  )
  
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "T1a", tab1a_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("T1a"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("T1a", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("T1a", "source"),
    notes    = passthrough_notes("T1a"))
}

# Table 1b: IPI Passthrough (latest month) - main text
if (ipi_available && exists("ipi_passthrough")) {
  tab1b_data <- ipi_passthrough %>%
    dplyr::select(Category,
                  "Import share" = "Import Share",
                  "Tariff increase (pp)" = "Tariff Increase",
                  "Expected price change under 100% passthrough" = "Expected Effect",
                  "Price change, 2025 change" = "2025 Change",
                  "Price change, vs LP trend" = "vs LP Trend",
                  "Price change, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear",
                  "Passthrough, via 2025 change" = "2025 Change ",
                  "Passthrough, vs LP trend" =  "vs LP Trend ",
                  "Passthrough, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear "
                  )
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "T1b", tab1b_data,
    title    = paste0(fig_excel_title("T1b"), " (", data_month, ")"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("T1b", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("T1b", "source"),
    notes    = passthrough_notes("T1b"))
}

# Table A1: All-PCE Passthrough (June 2025) - appendix
tblA1_data <- passthrough_data_jun %>%
  dplyr::select(Category,
                "Import share" = "Import Share",
                "Tariff increase (pp)" = "Tariff Increase",
                "Expected price change under 100% passthrough" = "Expected Effect",
                "Price change, 2025 change" = "2025 Change",
                "Price change, vs LP trend" = "vs LP Trend",
                "Price change, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear",
                "Passthrough, via 2025 change" = "2025 Change ",
                "Passthrough, vs LP trend" = "vs LP Trend ",
                "Passthrough, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear "
                )
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "TA1", tblA1_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("TA1"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("TA1", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("TA1", "source"))

# Table A2: All-PCE Passthrough (latest month) - appendix
tblA2_data <- passthrough_data %>%
  dplyr::select(Category,
                "Import share" = "Import Share",
                "Tariff increase (pp)" = "Tariff Increase",
                "Expected price change under 100% passthrough" = "Expected Effect",
                "Price change, 2025 change" = "2025 Change",
                "Price change, vs LP trend" = "vs LP Trend",
                "Price change, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear",
                "Passthrough, via 2025 change" = "2025 Change ",
                "Passthrough, vs LP trend" = "vs LP Trend ",
                "Passthrough, vs Log-Linear" = "vs Log-Linear "
                )
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "TA2", tblA2_data,
  title    = paste0(fig_excel_title("TA2"), " (", data_month, ")"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("TA2", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("TA2", "source"))

# Figures 7-8: Imported PCE Price Index (conditional)
if (ipi_available && exists("ipi_combined")) {
  fig5_data <- ipi_combined %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
           "Imported core goods index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = core_goods_idx,
           "Imported core goods trend (pre-2025)" = core_trend,
           "Imported core goods trend, lower bound" = core_lower,
           "Imported core goods trend, upper bound" = core_upper,
           "Imported durables index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = durables_idx,
           "Imported durables trend (pre-2025)" = dur_trend,
           "Imported durables trend, lower bound" = dur_lower,
           "Imported durables trend, upper bound" = dur_upper,
           "Forecast indicator" = is_forecast)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F7", fig5_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F7"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F7", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F7", "source"))

  fig6_data <- ipi_for_fig6 %>%
    filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
           "Imported core goods deviation from trend (%)" = core_goods_vs_trend,
           "Imported durables deviation from trend (%)" = durables_vs_trend)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F8", fig6_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F8"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F8", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F8", "source"))
}

# Figure 9: Import Prices
fig7_data <- import_trend %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                "Import price index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = import_idx,
                "Trend" = trend,
                "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F9", fig7_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F9"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F9", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F9", "source"),
  notes    = fig_meta("F9", "notes"))

# Figure 10: Tariff Employment Index (conditional)
if (tariff_emp_available && exists("trend_recent")) {
  fig8_data <- trend_recent %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Employment index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = actual,
                  "Trend" = trend,
                  "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F10", fig8_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F10"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F10", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F10", "source"))
}

# Figure 11: Manufacturing Employment Index (conditional)
if (tariff_emp_mfg_available && exists("trend_mfg_recent")) {
  fig9_data <- trend_mfg_recent %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Manufacturing employment index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = actual,
                  "Trend" = trend,
                  "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F11", fig9_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F11"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F11", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F11", "source"))
}

# Employment Index Summary Table (conditional)
if (tariff_emp_available && tariff_emp_mfg_available && exists("emp_index_summary")) {
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "T_Emp", emp_index_summary,
    title    = fig_excel_title("T_Emp"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("T_Emp", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("T_Emp", "source"))
}

# Figure 12: Industrial Production
fig10_data <- ip_trend %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                "Industrial production index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = ip_idx,
                "Trend" = trend,
                "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F12", fig10_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F12"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F12", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F12", "source"))

# Figure 13: Exchange Rates (Indexed)
if (exists("fx_daily_indexed")) {
  fig11_data <- fx_daily_indexed %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = currency, values_from = index) %>%
    dplyr::rename("Date" = date)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F13", fig11_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("F13"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("F13", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("F13", "source"),
    date_format = "yyyy-mm-dd")
}

# Figure 14: Real Imports and Exports (2025 dollars)
fig12_data <- trade_with_trend %>%
  dplyr::select(date,
         imports_real_2025, imports_trend_2025,
         exports_real_2025, exports_trend_2025) %>%
  mutate(
    imports_vs_trend = (imports_real_2025 / imports_trend_2025 - 1) * 100,
    exports_vs_trend = (exports_real_2025 / exports_trend_2025 - 1) * 100
  ) %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    "Date" = date,
    "Real imports (millions 2025 USD)" = imports_real_2025,
    "Imports trend (millions 2025 USD)" = imports_trend_2025,
    "Real exports (millions 2025 USD)" = exports_real_2025,
    "Exports trend (millions 2025 USD)" = exports_trend_2025,
    "Imports deviation from trend (%)" = imports_vs_trend,
    "Exports deviation from trend (%)" = exports_vs_trend
  )
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F14", fig12_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F14"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F14", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F14", "source"))

# Figure 15: Trade Deviation + Cumulative Trade Gap
fig13_data <- trade_with_trend %>%
  dplyr::select(date, imports_real, imports_trend, exports_real, exports_trend) %>%
  mutate(
    imports_vs_trend_pct = (imports_real / imports_trend - 1) * 100,
    exports_vs_trend_pct = (exports_real / exports_trend - 1) * 100
  ) %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    "Date" = date,
    "Real imports" = imports_real,
    "Imports trend" = imports_trend,
    "Real exports" = exports_real,
    "Exports trend" = exports_trend,
    "Imports deviation from trend (%)" = imports_vs_trend_pct,
    "Exports deviation from trend (%)" = exports_vs_trend_pct
  )
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F15a", fig13_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F15a"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F15a", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F15a", "source"))

fig13b_data <- cumulative_imports %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                "Real imports" = imports_real,
                "Imports trend" = imports_trend,
                "Monthly gap (billions USD)" = monthly_gap,
                "Cumulative gap (billions USD)" = cumulative_gap)
add_tbl_sheet(wb, "F15b", fig13b_data,
  title    = fig_excel_title("F15b"),
  subtitle = fig_meta("F15b", "subtitle"),
  source   = fig_meta("F15b", "source"))

# Figure A1: PCE Prices with Log-Linear Trend
if (exists("ll_combined")) {
  figA1_data <- ll_combined %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
           "Core goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = core_goods_idx,
           "Core goods trend (log-linear)" = core_trend,
           "Core goods trend, lower bound" = core_lower,
           "Core goods trend, upper bound" = core_upper,
           "Durable goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = durables_idx,
           "Durable goods trend (log-linear)" = dur_trend,
           "Durable goods trend, lower bound" = dur_lower,
           "Durable goods trend, upper bound" = dur_upper,
           "Forecast indicator" = is_forecast)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "FA1", figA1_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("FA1"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("FA1", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("FA1", "source"))
}

# Figure A2: Deviation from Log-Linear Trend
if (exists("ll_deviations")) {
  figA2_data <- ll_deviations %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Core goods deviation from trend (%)" = core_goods_vs_trend,
                  "Durable goods deviation from trend (%)" = durables_vs_trend)
  add_tbl_sheet(wb, "FA2", figA2_data,
    title    = fig_excel_title("FA2"),
    subtitle = fig_meta("FA2", "subtitle"),
    source   = fig_meta("FA2", "source"))
}

# === Data TOC Sheet ===
addWorksheet(wb, "Data TOC", gridLines = FALSE)

# Row 1: Report title (bold)
writeData(wb, "Data TOC", "Tracking the Economic Effects of Tariffs",
          startRow = 1, startCol = 1)
addStyle(wb, "Data TOC", titleStyle, rows = 1, cols = 1)

# Row 2: Generated date
writeData(wb, "Data TOC", format(Sys.Date(), "%B %Y"),
          startRow = 2, startCol = 1)
addStyle(wb, "Data TOC", metaStyle, rows = 2, cols = 1)

# Row 3: Organization
writeData(wb, "Data TOC", "The Budget Lab at Yale",
          startRow = 3, startCol = 1)
addStyle(wb, "Data TOC", metaStyle, rows = 3, cols = 1)

# Row 4: blank

# Row 5: Section header (bold)
writeData(wb, "Data TOC", "Tables and Figures",
          startRow = 5, startCol = 1)
addStyle(wb, "Data TOC", titleStyle, rows = 5, cols = 1)

# Rows 6+: Hyperlinked list of sheets
for (i in seq_len(nrow(sheet_info))) {
  link_formula <- paste0("HYPERLINK(\"#'", sheet_info$sheet_name[i],
                         "'!A1\", \"", sheet_info$title[i], "\")")
  writeFormula(wb, "Data TOC", x = link_formula,
               startRow = 5 + i, startCol = 1)
  addStyle(wb, "Data TOC", tocLinkStyle, rows = 5 + i, cols = 1)
}

setColWidths(wb, "Data TOC", cols = 1, widths = 80)

# Move Data TOC to first position
worksheetOrder(wb) <- c(length(names(wb)), 1:(length(names(wb)) - 1))

# Create filename with date
excel_filename <- paste0("tariff_impacts_results_", format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d"), ".xlsx")
excel_path <- file.path(OUTPUT_DIR, excel_filename)

# Save workbook
saveWorkbook(wb, excel_path, overwrite = TRUE)

# Count sheets for summary
n_sheets <- length(names(wb))
```

The raw data underlying all figures and tables has been exported to: **`r excel_filename`**

This Excel file contains `r n_sheets` sheets:

- **Data TOC**: Table of contents with hyperlinks to each data sheet
- **Fig1-Fig15**: Raw data for each figure (indexed values, trends, confidence intervals)
- **Tables**: Passthrough calculations and employment index summaries

---

## Export Website Assets

```{r export-website, include=FALSE}
# ==============================================================================
# WEBSITE EXPORT
# ==============================================================================
# Produces three outputs in website/:
#   1. csv/      — one CSV per figure/table (current, overwritten each run)
#   2. html/     — stripped-down HTML with narrative text and figure placeholders
#   3. vintages/ — date-stamped snapshots of csv/ and html/ for each run
# ==============================================================================

WEBSITE_DIR  <- here("website")
CSV_DIR      <- file.path(WEBSITE_DIR, "csv")
HTML_DIR     <- file.path(WEBSITE_DIR, "html")
VINTAGE_DATE <- format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d")
VINTAGE_DIR  <- file.path(WEBSITE_DIR, "vintages", VINTAGE_DATE)
VINTAGE_CSV  <- file.path(VINTAGE_DIR, "csv")
VINTAGE_HTML <- file.path(VINTAGE_DIR, "html")
dir.create(CSV_DIR,      showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
dir.create(HTML_DIR,     showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
dir.create(VINTAGE_CSV,  showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
dir.create(VINTAGE_HTML, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

# --- Helper: write a CSV with metadata header rows (current + vintage) ---
write_website_csv <- function(data, filename, fig_id) {
  path <- file.path(CSV_DIR, filename)
  tryCatch({
    # Write metadata as comment lines, then data
    meta_lines <- c(
      paste0("# ", fig_meta(fig_id, "label"), ". ", fig_meta(fig_id, "title")),
      paste0("# ", fig_meta(fig_id, "subtitle")),
      paste0("# Source: ", fig_meta(fig_id, "source"))
    )
    notes <- fig_meta(fig_id, "notes")
    if (nzchar(notes)) meta_lines <- c(meta_lines, paste0("# Note: ", notes))
    writeLines(meta_lines, path)
    write.table(data, path, append = TRUE, sep = ",", row.names = FALSE,
                quote = TRUE, na = "")
    # Copy to vintage
    file.copy(path, file.path(VINTAGE_CSV, filename), overwrite = TRUE)
    TRUE
  }, error = function(e) {
    message(paste("Could not write CSV", filename, ":", e$message))
    FALSE
  })
}

# --- CSV exports (mirrors Excel export section) ---

# F1: Customs Duty Revenue
write_website_csv(fig1_data, "F1_customs_duty_revenue.csv", "F1")

# F2: Effective Tariff Rate
write_website_csv(fig2_data, "F2_effective_tariff_rate.csv", "F2")

# F3: Daily ETR
if (daily_etr_available && exists("fig3_data")) {
  write_website_csv(fig3_data, "F3_daily_etr.csv", "F3")
}

# F4: Daily ETR by Authority
if (daily_etr_available && exists("fig4_data")) {
  write_website_csv(fig4_data, "F4_daily_etr_by_authority.csv", "F4")
}

# F5: PCE Prices with LP Trend
fig5_csv <- pce_combined %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
         "Core goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = core_goods_idx,
         "Core goods trend (pre-2025)" = core_trend,
         "Core goods trend, lower bound" = core_lower,
         "Core goods trend, upper bound" = core_upper,
         "Durable goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = durables_idx,
         "Durable goods trend (pre-2025)" = dur_trend,
         "Durable goods trend, lower bound" = dur_lower,
         "Durable goods trend, upper bound" = dur_upper,
         "Forecast indicator" = is_forecast)
write_website_csv(fig5_csv, "F5_pce_prices_lp_trend.csv", "F5")

# F6: PCE Deviation from Trend
fig6_csv <- pce_for_fig4 %>%
  filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                "Core goods deviation from trend (%)" = core_goods_vs_trend,
                "Durable goods deviation from trend (%)" = dur_vs_trend)
write_website_csv(fig6_csv, "F6_pce_deviation_from_trend.csv", "F6")

# T1a: IPI Passthrough (June 2025)
if (ipi_available && exists("ipi_passthrough_jun")) {
  write_website_csv(tab1a_data, "T1a_ipi_passthrough_jun2025.csv", "T1a")
}

# T1b: IPI Passthrough (latest)
if (ipi_available && exists("ipi_passthrough")) {
  write_website_csv(tab1b_data, "T1b_ipi_passthrough_latest.csv", "T1b")
}

# F7: Imported PCE Prices
if (ipi_available && exists("ipi_combined")) {
  fig7_csv <- ipi_combined %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
           "Imported core goods index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = core_goods_idx,
           "Imported core goods trend (pre-2025)" = core_trend,
           "Imported core goods trend, lower bound" = core_lower,
           "Imported core goods trend, upper bound" = core_upper,
           "Imported durables index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = durables_idx,
           "Imported durables trend (pre-2025)" = dur_trend,
           "Imported durables trend, lower bound" = dur_lower,
           "Imported durables trend, upper bound" = dur_upper,
           "Forecast indicator" = is_forecast)
  write_website_csv(fig7_csv, "F7_imported_pce_prices.csv", "F7")

  fig8_csv <- ipi_for_fig6 %>%
    filter(date >= "2024-12-01") %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
           "Imported core goods deviation from trend (%)" = core_goods_vs_trend,
           "Imported durables deviation from trend (%)" = durables_vs_trend)
  write_website_csv(fig8_csv, "F8_imported_pce_deviation.csv", "F8")
}

# F9: Import Prices
fig9_csv <- import_trend %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                "Import price index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = import_idx,
                "Trend" = trend,
                "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
write_website_csv(fig9_csv, "F9_import_prices.csv", "F9")

# F10: Employment Index
if (tariff_emp_available && exists("trend_recent")) {
  fig10_csv <- trend_recent %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Employment index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = actual,
                  "Trend" = trend,
                  "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
  write_website_csv(fig10_csv, "F10_employment_index.csv", "F10")
}

# F11: Manufacturing Employment Index
if (tariff_emp_mfg_available && exists("trend_mfg_recent")) {
  fig11_csv <- trend_mfg_recent %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Manufacturing employment index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = actual,
                  "Trend" = trend,
                  "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
  write_website_csv(fig11_csv, "F11_manufacturing_employment_index.csv", "F11")
}

# T_Emp: Employment Summary
if (tariff_emp_available && tariff_emp_mfg_available && exists("emp_index_summary")) {
  write_website_csv(emp_index_summary, "T_Emp_employment_summary.csv", "T_Emp")
}

# F12: Industrial Production
fig12_csv <- ip_trend %>%
  dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                "Industrial production index (Dec 2024 = 100)" = ip_idx,
                "Trend" = trend,
                "Deviation from trend (%)" = vs_trend)
write_website_csv(fig12_csv, "F12_industrial_production.csv", "F12")

# F13: Exchange Rates
if (exists("fx_daily_indexed")) {
  fig13_csv <- fx_daily_indexed %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = currency, values_from = index) %>%
    dplyr::rename("Date" = date)
  write_website_csv(fig13_csv, "F13_exchange_rates.csv", "F13")
}

# F14: Real Imports and Exports
write_website_csv(fig12_data, "F14_real_imports_exports.csv", "F14")

# F15a: Trade Deviation from Trend
write_website_csv(fig13_data, "F15a_trade_deviation.csv", "F15a")

# F15b: Cumulative Import Gap
write_website_csv(fig13b_data, "F15b_cumulative_import_gap.csv", "F15b")

# TA1: All-PCE Passthrough (June 2025)
write_website_csv(tblA1_data, "TA1_pce_passthrough_jun2025.csv", "TA1")

# TA2: All-PCE Passthrough (latest)
write_website_csv(tblA2_data, "TA2_pce_passthrough_latest.csv", "TA2")

# FA1: PCE Prices with Log-Linear Trend
if (exists("ll_combined")) {
  figA1_csv <- ll_combined %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
           "Core goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = core_goods_idx,
           "Core goods trend (log-linear)" = core_trend,
           "Core goods trend, lower bound" = core_lower,
           "Core goods trend, upper bound" = core_upper,
           "Durable goods index (December 2024 = 100)" = durables_idx,
           "Durable goods trend (log-linear)" = dur_trend,
           "Durable goods trend, lower bound" = dur_lower,
           "Durable goods trend, upper bound" = dur_upper,
           "Forecast indicator" = is_forecast)
  write_website_csv(figA1_csv, "FA1_pce_prices_loglinear_trend.csv", "FA1")
}

# FA2: Deviation from Log-Linear Trend
if (exists("ll_deviations")) {
  figA2_csv <- ll_deviations %>%
    dplyr::select("Date" = date,
                  "Core goods deviation from trend (%)" = core_goods_vs_trend,
                  "Durable goods deviation from trend (%)" = durables_vs_trend)
  write_website_csv(figA2_csv, "FA2_pce_deviation_loglinear.csv", "FA2")
}

website_csv_count <- length(list.files(CSV_DIR, pattern = "\\.csv$"))

# --- Stripped-down HTML with narrative text and figure placeholders ---

# Helper: build a figure/table placeholder div
fig_placeholder <- function(fig_id, csv_file = NULL) {
  meta <- FIGURE_METADATA[[fig_id]]
  label <- meta$label
  title <- meta$title
  subtitle <- if (!is.null(meta$subtitle)) meta$subtitle else ""
  csv_attr <- if (!is.null(csv_file)) paste0(' data-csv="', csv_file, '"') else ""
  paste0(
    '<div class="figure-placeholder" id="', tolower(gsub("[^A-Za-z0-9]", "-", fig_id)),
    '" data-figure-id="', fig_id, '"', csv_attr, '>\n',
    '  <p class="figure-label">', label, '. ', title, '</p>\n',
    if (nzchar(subtitle)) paste0('  <p class="figure-subtitle">', subtitle, '</p>\n') else "",
    '</div>\n'
  )
}

# Build HTML content
html_parts <- list()

html_parts$header <- paste0(
  '<article class="tariff-impacts-report">\n',
  '<p class="report-date">Updated: ', format(Sys.Date(), "%B %d, %Y"), '</p>\n\n'
)

# --- Key Takeaways ---
html_parts$takeaways <- paste0(
  '<section id="key-takeaways">\n',
  '<h2>Key Takeaways</h2>\n',
  '<ul>\n',
  '<li><strong>Updated economic indicators:</strong> This report reviews economic indicators that are related to the effect of Tariffs on the US Economy as of ', format_date_long(), '. For the most part, given data lags, the findings reflect the conditions prior to the SCOTUS decision repealing IEEPA Tariffs on February 20th, 2026.</li>\n',
  '<li><strong>Tariff revenue:</strong> The 2025 tariffs have raised an estimated $', safe_fmt(total_excess_rev), ' billion in inflation-adjusted customs revenue above the 2022&ndash;2024 average as of ', latest_month_name, ', with the effective tariff rate reaching ', safe_fmt(latest_tariff_rate), '% in ', latest_tariff_month, '.</li>\n',
  '<li><strong>Pass-through to consumer prices:</strong> Imported PCE core goods and durable goods prices have risen ', safe_fmt(tk_ipi_ytd_core), '% and ', safe_fmt(tk_ipi_ytd_dur), '% respectively during 2025 through ', data_month_name, ', both well above prior-year comparisons. Implied passthrough of tariffs to imported consumer goods prices ranges from roughly ', safe_fmt(tk_ipi_core_low, "%.0f"), '&ndash;', safe_fmt(tk_ipi_core_high, "%.0f"), '% for core goods and ', safe_fmt(tk_ipi_dur_low, "%.0f"), '&ndash;', safe_fmt(tk_ipi_dur_high, "%.0f"), '% for durables, depending on methodology (see Tables 1a&ndash;1b).</li>\n',
  '<li><strong>Employment:</strong> There is no definitive indication of a significant aggregate labor market effect so far, though tariff-exposed industries show some signs of weakness relative to pre-2025 trend.</li>\n',
  '</ul>\n',
  '</section>\n\n'
)

# --- Introduction ---
html_parts$intro <- paste0(
  '<section id="introduction">\n',
  '<h2>Introduction</h2>\n',
  '<p>In response to new tariff announcements, The Budget Lab (TBL) regularly estimates fiscal and macroeconomic effects resulting from policy; TBL published its latest report on <a href="https://budgetlab.yale.edu/research/state-tariffs-february-21-2026">February 21st</a>, and we aim to update these projections as we refine our methodology and as policies change. These analyses rely on projections from well-respected economic and trade models, including the MA/US macroeconometric model (currently maintained by S&amp;P Global) for short-run analysis and the Global Trade Analysis Project (GTAP) model for medium/long-run analysis.</p>\n',
  '<p>One year after the initial wave of new tariffs, this report updates a <a href="https://budgetlab.yale.edu/research/short-run-effects-2025-tariffs-so-far">September 2025</a> assessment of how actual economic indicators have responded to tariff changes. This provides a check on the assumptions made by economic models, as well as a real-time assessment of how tariffs are impacting the US economy. Most of these indicators are updated monthly, and going forward this report will be released on that same schedule. In particular, we consider the impact of tariffs on revenues, prices, trade, and labor markets. Critically, these are not causal estimates of the effect of tariffs on the economy, but rather constitute a descriptive attempt to explore how the economy is changing with tariffs. There are a number of other economic changes happening concurrently, including but not limited to the growth of generative AI and the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which we do not control for in the series presented below. This report was updated using data as of ', format_date_long(), '.</p>\n',
  '<p>Overall, there is evidence, consistent with economic theory, that tariffs have raised both additional revenue ($', safe_fmt(total_excess_rev), ' Billion above the 2022&ndash;2024 average) and led to higher prices (Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) core goods up ', safe_fmt(core_ytd_change), '% during 2025 through ', data_month_name, '). There is less clear evidence about the impacts of tariffs on the wider economy: for example, employment in tariff-exposed industries is not appreciably lower or higher than what would have been expected prior to 2025. Importantly, these statistics are representations of the current economy, and it is difficult to disentangle what changed due to tariffs versus the many other economic changes over the past year. It is still early and the effects of tariffs may evolve and change over time as consumers, businesses, and policymakers respond. Importantly, with the exception of currency data, we do not yet have outcomes that reflect the economic reality after the Supreme Court decision rolling back the President&rsquo;s authority to use the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs.</p>\n',
  '</section>\n\n'
)

# --- Revenues & Effective Tariff Rates ---
html_parts$revenue <- paste0(
  '<section id="revenues-effective-tariff-rates">\n',
  '<h2>Revenues &amp; Effective Tariff Rates</h2>\n',
  '<p>Tariffs are a tax on imports, therefore higher tariffs will increase revenues if their effect on imports is not sufficiently prohibitive. Before the 2025 tariff announcements, the average effective US tariff rate was ', safe_fmt(pre_2025_tariff_rate), '% (2022&ndash;2024 average), raising $', safe_fmt(pre_2025_monthly_rev), ' billion a month in net customs duty revenues. The new 2025 tariffs have pushed the average effective US rate to ', safe_fmt(latest_tariff_rate), '%, and have raised roughly $', safe_fmt(total_excess_rev), ' billion in revenues so far (after accounting for the 2022&ndash;2024 average), with $', safe_fmt(excess_rev_2025_only), ' billion collected over the course of 2025 and about $', safe_fmt(latest_month_rev), ' billion of that new tariff revenue posted in ', latest_month_name, ' alone.</p>\n',
  '<p>However, the revenue landscape has shifted significantly. On February 20th, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled 6&ndash;3 in <em>Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump</em> that the use of IEEPA to impose tariffs exceeded presidential authority, striking down all IEEPA-based tariffs&mdash;including the reciprocal, fentanyl, and universal baseline tariff regimes. Tariffs imposed using other authorities remain in effect, and the questions of legality and implementation of IEEPA refunds remains uncertain. This means that some or all of the IEEPA revenue collected to date may ultimately be returned to importers. Our back-of-the-envelope estimate, projecting forward using the average of observed FY 2026 CBP collections as of December 14th (the same methodology behind our earlier $142 billion figure), puts total IEEPA revenue at roughly $168 billion through February 19th, 2026. The customs revenue data presented in this report do not yet cover the post-SCOTUS period, and substantial uncertainty remains: the ultimate fiscal impact will depend on the resolution of refund litigation, the duration and scope of the replacement Section 122 tariffs, and any further legislative or executive action.</p>\n',
  '<p>The tariff revenue and rate numbers reported here are a different measure than the numbers reported in TBL&rsquo;s State of Tariff Report. The effective tariff rates (ETRs) reported here are actual tariff duties divided by the value of customs, meaning that they incorporate any avoidance behavior by firms, importers, or other actors. The effective tariff rates reported in TBL&rsquo;s State of Tariff Report are model-based estimates of the average rates across sectors, weighted by 2024 trade flows. In that report, we include two measures of average ETRs: pre-substitution and post-substitution. In our modeling of the general revenue effects of tariffs, our last pre-SCOTUS estimate of tariff revenue was projected to raise $246 billion in annual revenue for fiscal year (FY) 2026 (conventionally). This is lower than the annualized revenue we would expect if monthly tariff revenue remained at its current level (2-month average of ', annualized_rev_months, ' projected forward) for the rest of the fiscal year ($', safe_fmt(annualized_rev, "%.0f"), ' billion). However, the revenue estimates in the State of Tariff Report account for decreases in other revenue sources, like federal payroll and corporate income taxes, following the methodology of the <a href="https://www.cbo.gov/publication/58549">U.S. Congressional Budget Office</a>. Adjusting for that gives us a simulated annual revenue of $319 billion, notably closer to the annualized tariff revenue level suggested by the customs data.</p>\n',
  '\n',
  fig_placeholder("F1", "F1_customs_duty_revenue.csv"),
  fig_placeholder("F2", "F2_effective_tariff_rate.csv"),
  if (daily_etr_available) fig_placeholder("F3", "F3_daily_etr.csv") else "",
  if (daily_etr_available) fig_placeholder("F4", "F4_daily_etr_by_authority.csv") else "",
  '</section>\n\n'
)

# --- Prices and Consumer Passthrough ---
html_parts$prices <- paste0(
  '<section id="prices-consumer-passthrough">\n',
  '<h2>Prices and Consumer Passthrough</h2>\n',
  '<p>One of the most debated questions around tariffs is to what extent they are leading to higher consumer prices. Tariffs are taxes that add to the cost of imported goods. Different groups can bear this cost, or incidence: foreign producers (in the form of accepting lower prices on their exports), American importers and businesses (in the form of lower profit margins), and American consumers (in the form of lower real after-tax incomes). Moreover, tariffs reduce productivity and thereby real U.S. income (even when including tariff revenue) by reducing the efficiency of resource allocation across countries and increasing the marginal cost of investment. After-tax real incomes in turn can fall either from a rise in realized prices, holding nominal income constant, or a fall in nominal income.</p>\n',
  '\n',
  '<h3>Overall Goods Prices</h3>\n',
  '<p>One possible indication that tariffs are raising consumer goods prices is that goods prices have increased since the beginning of the year, both in absolute terms and against reasonable estimates of pre-2025 trend. We estimate the pre-2025 trend using a local projection method with recession and pandemic controls. See the <a href="', meth_link, '">', meth_text, '</a> for full details. We find similar differences when the trend is calculated using a simple log-linear approach. During 2025 through ', data_month_name, ', PCE core goods prices (goods excluding volatile food &amp; energy components) rose ', safe_fmt(core_ytd_change), '%, versus ', safe_fmt(core_2023_change), '% over the same ', months_since_2025, '-month period starting in 2023.
```
